# Experiments on hyper-*k*-mers {#sec-kfc-supplementary}

## Datasets {#sec-kfc-datasets}

@tbl-datasets lists the datasets used in the experiments and their characteristics.

:::{#tbl-datasets .large-table}
| Name | Type | Coverage | \# reads | Total length | Min. | Avg. | Max. |
|:--|:--:|--:|--:|---:|-:|--:|--:|
| `SRR11434954` | HiFi | 5000× | 1,789,131 | 23,122,913,014 | 46 | 12,924.1 | 26,294 |
| `SRR28370642` | ONT Duplex | 50× | 114,703 | 236,908,842 | 349 | 2,065.4 | 126,029 |
| `SRR28370651` | ONT Duplex | 50× | 109,061 | 226,502,819 | 330 | 2,076.8 | 125,012 |
| `SRR28370668` | ONT Simplex | 2000× | 6,819,683 | 9,101,103,830 | 1 | 1,334.5 | 396,011 |

E. Coli datasets used for benchmarking KFC. Min., Avg. and Max. refer to the minimum, average and maximum read length respectively.
:::

## Multi-threading efficiency of KFC {#sec-kfc-multithread}

In this section, we assess the multi-threading efficiency of KFC (@fig-plot-ont-100x-multithread).
Our results demonstrate that KFC effectively utilizes multi-core architectures, achieving performance gains with up to several dozen cores before experiencing diminishing returns.

:::{#fig-plot-ont-100x-multithread}
![](../fig/hkmer/plots/MTecolihifi.png){width=80%}

Multi-core usage efficiency of KFC on the 100× HiFi E. coli dataset.
:::

## Metagenomic benchmarks {#sec-kfc-metagenomic}

@fig-plot-zymo-hifi and @fig-plot-gut-hifi confirm the trends of @sec-kfc-longreads on two larger HiFi metagenomic datasets.

:::{#fig-plot-zymo-hifi layout-ncol=2}
![Memory usage](../fig/hkmer/plots/zymohifi100xmem){#fig-sub-mem}

![Running time](../fig/hkmer/plots/zymohifi100xtime){#fig-sub-time}

/kmer counting benchmark on HiFi Zymo community dataset (SRR13128014) downsampled at 5 gigabases, with unique /kmer filtering.
:::

:::{#fig-plot-gut-hifi layout-ncol=2}
![Memory usage](../fig/hkmer/plots/guthifimem){#fig-sub-mem}

![Running time](../fig/hkmer/plots/guthifitime){#fig-sub-time}

/kmer counting benchmark on HiFi human gut datasets (SRR15275210, SRR15275211, SRR15275212, SRR15275213) downsampled at 15 gigabases, with unique /kmer filtering.
:::

## Effect of coverage {#sec-kfc-coverage}

@fig-combined-benchmark-coverage shows benchmarks similar to @fig-combined-benchmark-all but at various coverage levels, without significant change in the relative behaviors of the tools.

:::{#fig-combined-benchmark-coverage layout-ncol=2}
![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecolihifi50xmem)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecolihifi50xtime)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecoliont200xmem)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecoliont200xtime)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecoliont500xmem)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecoliont500xtime)

/kmer counting benchmark on ONT Simplex E. coli dataset (SRR28370668) for various coverage.
:::

:::{#fig-plot-gut-full layout-ncol=2}
![](../fig/hkmer/plots/gutfullmem)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/gutfulltime)

/kmer counting benchmark on the complete HiFi human gut datasets (SRR15275210, SRR15275211, SRR15275212, SRR15275213), filtering /kmers appearing once or twice (abundance threshold $t = 3$).
:::

## Effect of not filtering unique /kmers {#sec-kfc-nofilter}

In this section, we present the performance of KFC when unique /kmers are not filtered out.
@fig-combined-benchmark-nofilter shows the results discussed in @sec-kfc-longreads.
They are similar to @fig-combined-benchmark-all, but without filtering unique /kmers.

:::{#fig-combined-benchmark-nofilter layout-ncol=2}
![](../fig/hkmer/plots/duplexab1mem)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/duplexab1time)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecolihifi100xab1mem)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecolihifi100xab1time)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecoliont100xab1mem)

![](../fig/hkmer/plots/ecoliont100xab1time)

Comparison of /kmer benchmarks on different E. coli datasets without any filtering.
Each subfigure shows the memory usage and timing plots for different sequencing technologies.
:::

## Pangenome benchmark {#sec-kfc-pangenome}

We evaluate the tools on one thousand *S. enterica* complete genomes from NCBI to assess the cost of counting large /kmers across a pangenome (@fig-plot-salmo).
KFC remains the most memory-efficient tool ahead of FastK and KMC, and for very large /kmer sizes ($k > 500$) it also becomes the fastest, although its multithreading efficiency (analyzed in @sec-kfc-multithread) lags behind KMC on small input sizes, so it leads only when running on fewer threads.

:::{#fig-plot-salmo layout-ncol=2}
![Memory usage](../fig/hkmer/plots/salmomem){#fig-sub-mem}

![Running time](../fig/hkmer/plots/salmotime){#fig-sub-time}

/kmer counting benchmark on one thousand *S. enterica* complete genomes.
:::
